title: The Magicians of Caprona - Chapter 5 (Fragment 4)
text:
Marco came through the gate and stopped so dead, staring at Chrestomanci, that Rosa charged into him and nearly knocked him over. Marco staggered a bit, put his arms round Rosa, and went on staring at Chrestomanci. Tonino found himself holding his breath. Rinaldo was right. There was something about Marco. Chrestomanci knew it, and Marco knew he knew. From the look on Marco’s face, he expected Chrestomanci to say what it was.

Chrestomanci indeed opened his mouth to say something, but he shut it again and pursed his lips in a sort of whistle instead. Marco looked at him uncertainly.

“Oh,” said Uncle Umberto, “may I introduce—” He stopped and thought. Rosa he usually remembered, because of her fair hair, but he could not place Marco. “Corinna’s fiancé,” he suggested.

“I’m Rosa,” said Rosa. “This is Marco Andretti.”

“How do you do?” Chrestomanci said politely. Marco seemed to relax. Chrestomanci’s eyes turned to Paolo and Tonino, standing staring. “Good heavens!” he said. “Everyone here seems to live such exciting lives. What have you boys killed?”

Paolo and Tonino looked down in consternation, to find that the steak was leaking on to their shoes. Two or three cats were approaching meaningly.

Aunt Gina appeared in the kitchen doorway. “Where’s my steak?”

Paolo and Tonino sped towards her, leaving a pattering trail. “What was all that about?” Paolo panted to Tonino.

“I don’t know,” said Tonino, because he didn’t, and because he liked Marco.

Aunt Gina shortly became very sharp and passionate about the steak. The leaking trail attracted every cat in the Casa. They were underfoot in the kitchen all evening, mewing pitifully. Benvenuto was also present, at a wary distance from Aunt Gina, and he made good use of his time. Aunt Gina erupted into the yard again, trumpeting.

“Tonino! Ton-in-ooh! ”

Tonino laid down his book and hurried outside. “Yes, Aunt Gina?”

“That cat of yours has stolen a whole pound of steak!” Aunt Gina trumpeted, flinging a dramatic arm skyward.

Tonino looked, and there, sure enough, Benvenuto was, crouched on the pan-tiles of the roof, with one paw holding down quite a large lump of meat. “Oh dear,” he said. “I don’t think I can make him give it back, Aunt Gina.”

“I don’t want it back. Look where it’s been!” screamed Aunt Gina. “Tell him from me that I shall wring his evil neck if he comes near me again!”

“My goodness, you do seem to be at the center of everything,” Chrestomanci remarked, appearing beside Tonino in the yard. “Are you always in such demand?”

“I shall have hysterics,” declared Aunt Gina. “And no one will get any supper.” Elizabeth and Aunt Maria and Cousins Claudia and Teresa immediately came to her assistance and led her tenderly back indoors.

“Thank the Lord!” said Chrestomanci. “I’m not sure I could stand hysterics and starvation at once. How did you know I was an enchanter, Tonino? From Benvenuto?”

“No. I just knew when I looked at you,” said Tonino.

“I see,” said Chrestomanci. “This is interesting. Most people find it impossible to tell. It makes me wonder if Old Niccolo is right, when he talks of the virtue leaving your house. Would you be able to tell another enchanter when you looked at him, do you think?”

Tonino screwed up his face and wondered. “I might. It’s the eyes. You mean, would I know the enchanter who’s spoiling our spells?”

“I think I mean that,” said Chrestomanci. “I’m beginning to believe there is someone. I’m sure, at least, that the spells on the Old Bridge were deliberately broken. Would it interfere with your plans too much, if I asked your grandfather to take you with him whenever he has to meet strangers?”

“I haven’t got any plans,” said Tonino. Then he thought, and he laughed. “I think you make jokes all the time.”

“I aim to please,” Chrestomanci said.

However, when Tonino next saw Chrestomanci, it was at supper— which was magnificent, despite Benvenuto and the hysterics—and Chrestomanci was very serious indeed. “My dear Niccolo,” he said, “my mission has to concern the misuse of magic, not the balance of power in Italy. There would be no end of trouble if I was caught trying to stop a war.”

vocabulary.definition: the action of helping someone
vocabulary.example: ” Elizabeth and Aunt Maria and Cousins Claudia and Teresa immediately came to her assistance and led her tenderly back indoors.

vocabulary.definition: a condition in which different elements are equal or in correct proportions
vocabulary.example: “My dear Niccolo,” he said, “my mission has to concern the misuse of magic, not the balance of power in Italy.

vocabulary.definition: to be discovered doing something wrong or illegal
vocabulary.example: There would be no end of trouble if I was caught trying to stop a war.

vocabulary.definition: to rush forward and collide with someone or something
vocabulary.example: Marco came through the gate and stopped so dead, staring at Chrestomanci, that Rosa charged into him and nearly knocked him over.

vocabulary.definition: feelings of anxiety or dismay, typically at something unexpected
vocabulary.example:
”

Paolo and Tonino looked down in consternation, to find that the steak was leaking on to their shoes.

vocabulary.definition: consciously and intentionally; on purpose
vocabulary.example: I’m sure, at least, that the spells on the Old Bridge were deliberately broken.

vocabulary.definition: a person who uses magic or enchanting spells
vocabulary.example: How did you know I was an enchanter, Tonino?

vocabulary.definition: causing great enthusiasm and eagerness
vocabulary.example: “Everyone here seems to live such exciting lives.

vocabulary.definition: a man engaged to be married
vocabulary.example: “Corinna’s fiancé,” he suggested.

vocabulary.definition: an uncontrollable outburst of emotion or fear
vocabulary.example:
”

“I shall have hysterics,” declared Aunt Gina.

vocabulary.definition: to prevent a process or activity from continuing normally
vocabulary.example: Would it interfere with your plans too much, if I asked your grandfather to take you with him whenever he has to meet strangers?

vocabulary.definition: to present a person to another
vocabulary.example: “Oh,” said Uncle Umberto, “may I introduce—” He stopped and thought.

vocabulary.definition: making a high-pitched crying sound like a cat
vocabulary.example: They were underfoot in the kitchen all evening, mewing pitifully.

vocabulary.definition: an important task or duty
vocabulary.example: “My dear Niccolo,” he said, “my mission has to concern the misuse of magic, not the balance of power in Italy.

vocabulary.definition: the wrong or improper use of something
vocabulary.example: “My dear Niccolo,” he said, “my mission has to concern the misuse of magic, not the balance of power in Italy.

vocabulary.definition: showing or caused by strong feelings or beliefs
vocabulary.example: Aunt Gina shortly became very sharp and passionate about the steak.

vocabulary.definition: a sequence of light tapping sounds left by footsteps
vocabulary.example:
”

Paolo and Tonino sped towards her, leaving a pattering trail.

vocabulary.definition: to press lips tightly together
vocabulary.example: Chrestomanci indeed opened his mouth to say something, but he shut it again and pursed his lips in a sort of whistle instead.

vocabulary.definition: to become less tense or anxious
vocabulary.example: Marco seemed to relax.

vocabulary.definition: to damage or ruin the quality or effect of something
vocabulary.example: You mean, would I know the enchanter who’s spoiling our spells?

vocabulary.definition: walk or move unsteadily as if about to fall
vocabulary.example: Marco staggered a bit, put his arms round Rosa, and went on staring at Chrestomanci.

vocabulary.definition: looking fixedly with wide-open eyes
vocabulary.example: Marco came through the gate and stopped so dead, staring at Chrestomanci, that Rosa charged into him and nearly knocked him over.

vocabulary.definition: suffering or death caused by lack of food
vocabulary.example: “I’m not sure I could stand hysterics and starvation at once.

vocabulary.definition: to make a loud sound typically of a trumpet; here, to shout loudly
vocabulary.example: Aunt Gina erupted into the yard again, trumpeting.

vocabulary.definition: behavior showing high moral standards
vocabulary.example: It makes me wonder if Old Niccolo is right, when he talks of the virtue leaving your house.

vocabulary.definition: feeling or showing caution about possible dangers
vocabulary.example: Benvenuto was also present, at a wary distance from Aunt Gina, and he made good use of his time.
